perspectives.label: Runtime layers
perspectives.description: Revo runtime stack as documented in docs/architecture-overview.md (CLI -> host -> engine/DBOS -> Revisium data-access -> execution) and the boundaries in docs/repo-layer-contract.md.

groups.label: Front doors (transport)
groups.description: MCP (agent front door) and GraphQL/HTTP (UI/script front door). Must stay thin: no raw control-plane table access, no DBOS access.

groups.label: Host & product services
groups.description: NestJS host daemon, product services, run lifecycle, pipeline wiring, pollers, task control-plane, observability.

groups.label: Engine (durable)
groups.description: DBOS adapter and the engine-agnostic pipeline interpreter. The engine boundary is thin (architecture-overview invariant 2).

groups.label: Revisium data-access
groups.description: The only layer that knows control-plane table shapes (repo-layer-contract). Shared contracts (errors, change-notifications) are allowed for transport; the data-access internals are not.

groups.label: Execution (runners)
groups.description: Short-lived Claude/Codex/script/integrator processes and worktree/process plumbing run against target repos.

constraints.message: Transport (MCP/GraphQL) must stay thin: no raw control-plane data-access. Use product services; shared contracts (control-plane/errors.ts, control-plane/change-notifications.ts) are allowed. See docs/repo-layer-contract.md.
Transport must not access DBOS directly; it calls product services. See docs/repo-layer-contract.md.
The engine boundary is thin: runners do not depend on DBOS internals. See docs/architecture-overview.md invariant 2.
Data-access does not depend on DBOS internals; DBOS progress is reached through the engine adapter from the host. See docs/architecture-overview.md invariants 2 and 5.
